attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man is brought into the Emergency Department via ambulance, after being found wandering around the street in a dazed and confused state. He is unable to provide a clear history, however a telephone discussion with his General Practitioner reveals a medical history of type 2 diabetes and chronic back pain. His medications include metformin, and he has recently been commenced on tramadol.

On examination he appears confused. His pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 105/76 mmHg, temperature 36.1°C, respiratory rate 16/min and SpO2 98% on room air. He is alert to voice, but not orientated to time or place. He is able to open his eyes when spoken to, however he appears drowsy. His speech is slurred and a musty odour is detected when he opens his mouth. A flapping tremor is noted on inspection of the hands, along with palmar erythema. Abdominal examination reveals moderate distension and shifting dullness, with no tenderness or guarding. Digital rectal examination demonstrates an empty rectum. His BGL is 6.5 mmol/L.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: 1000ml 0.9% sodium chloride IV STAT + 0.1 units/kg/hr Novorapid IV
attributes.explanation:
In this particular case, the underlying diagnosis is acute hepatic encephalopathy. Clues in the history that favour this diagnosis, include new-onset confusion and recent tramadol use (which can precipitate acute hepatic encephalopathy). Features on his examination are also in keeping with this diagnosis, including slurred speech and various signs of chronic liver disease (fetor hepaticus, flapping tremor, abdominal distension and shifting dullness).

Hepatic encephalopathy is thought to arise through a complex interplay between ammonia-induced neurotoxicity and excessive activation of GABAnergic neurotransmitter activity (inhibitory effect) in the brain. In cirrhosis, excessive ammonia levels (observed in up to 80% of those with hepatic encephalopathy) are related to impaired glutamine metabolism by the liver, as well as defective catabolism of nitrogenous sources (e.g. dietary proteins) in the gut.

Lactulose is an effective treatment for both acute and chronic hepatic encephalopathy, as it reduces ammonia absorption in the gut, as well as altering the colonic balance of ammonia-producing bacteria. Initial therapy in acute hepatic encephalopathy involves giving high dose lactulose (30-45ml once hourly) until the patient experiences a bowel motion. The dose and frequency can slowly be reduced thereafter, aiming to achieve 3-4 bowel actions everyday.

Rifaxamin is more expensive than lactulose and is considered a second-line agent in the management of hepatic encephalopathy, with various PBS criteria restricting the use of this agent. A combination of lactulose and rifaxamin may be suitable in patients who have achieved an inadequate response to lactulose alone.

There is no evidence that this patient is suffering from acute alcohol withdrawal, as he is drowsy, rather than agitated. Due to this, a benzodiazepine such as lorazepam does not have a role in his management.

This patient does not have a history of type 1 diabetes, which makes a diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) fairly unlikely. As such, the DKA protocol (which includes 1000ml 0.9% sodium chloride IV STAT + 0.1 units/kg/hr Novorapid IV) is not the correct management strategy.

Whilst there is a suggestion of recent tramadol use, the patient is not heavily sedated and his respiratory rate and oxygen saturation are normal. Therefore, opioid reversal (eg. naloxone 100mcg IV every 2 minutes) is not indicated at this time.
learningPoints: Hepatic encephalopathy is thought to arise through a complex interplay between ammonia-induced neurotoxicity and excessive activation of GABAnergic neurotransmitter activity (inhibitory effect) in the brain.
references: Frontera JA. Management of hepatic encephalopathy. Current Treat Options Neurol. 2014;16(6):1-37. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24807164/#:~:text=Liver%20transplantation%20is%20the%20definitive,remain%20a%20mainstay%20of%20therapy.
